caveats: a: No six-digit occupation mix for the narrowed 541990 lens was found; the published 541900 occupation table also contains photographic and veterinary services.
a: The DOE examples are site-specific demonstrations and do not establish average labor savings across commercial firms.
a: The injected compensation ratio uses 2024 wages and 2022 receipts, so its level has a stated vintage mismatch outside this primitive.
rho: Research pilots and large global testing companies have more technical resources than typical firms in the lens.
rho: Implementation rates will differ sharply by asset accessibility, inspection modality, and customer regulation.
e: The Census industry definition lists activities but does not measure their establishment or revenue shares.
e: The injected count of 863 is estimated using a broad Business and Consumer Services margin bridge and may not represent the margin profile of inspection firms.
e: Firms may use 541990 for government contracting even when their underlying activity fits another industry, increasing classification noise.
s5: Gallup covers all US employer businesses rather than inspection firms and measures plans rather than completed transactions.
s5: EY covers the global testing, inspection, and certification sector, which includes larger laboratories and certification businesses outside the lens.
s5: The eligible population is only an estimated subset of the injected industry count.
q: No source directly measures five-year retention of AI-derived benefits for independent US inspection providers.
q: Pricing power varies between emergency or specialized work and competitively bid routine patrols.
q: The global large-company cost pass-through evidence may overstate lower-middle-market bargaining power.
d5: Pipeline mileage is an installed-base measure, not purchased outsourced inspection quantity.
d5: The EY outlook is global, broader than the lens, and nominal rather than constant-price demand.
d5: The narrowed lens spans end markets with different construction and maintenance cycles.
o: Construction and building inspectors are not the same population as 541990 infrastructure and commodity inspectors.
o: Some routine visual patrol quantity may become fully automated or self-served by asset owners.
o: Operator need is highest in safety-critical regulated work and lower in low-consequence repeat observations.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: All Other Professional, Scientific, and Technical Services

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Infrastructure owners, industrial operators, utilities, commodity owners, insurers, and other businesses purchase documented inspections or verification under recurring service agreements or repeat work orders, commonly priced per visit, asset, route, project, or labor period.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 541990 is a residual code combining physical inspection, appraisal, dispute resolution, consumer counseling, brokerage, forecasting, and other unrelated services. The lens is narrowed to recurring business-to-business physical-asset inspection and technical verification because these activities share a coherent field-data, expert-review, and accountable-report workflow; the narrowing is for analytical coherence rather than attractiveness.
lens.excluded_activities: Appraisal, arbitration, conciliation, consumer credit counseling, credit repair, business and patent brokerage, weather forecasting, handwriting analysis, one-off expert assignments, captive internal inspection units, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms providing recurring or repeat outsourced physical-asset inspection and technical verification, including visual pipeline and power-line inspection, commodity inspection, and fire-suppression equipment testing or inspection without repair or installation.

a.bridge: BLS describes the tasks of construction and building inspectors rather than the narrowed 541990 population, and DOE and PHMSA document drone and machine-learning applications at selected infrastructure sites. I map remote data collection, image triage, planning, scheduling, log preparation, report drafting, and anomaly classification to the inspection workflow while leaving physical access, sensor setup, nuanced diagnosis, validation, and customer accountability largely unexposed. The BLS occupation is more building-focused and may overstate code review work, while demonstration projects may overstate feasible automation; the net direction of error is uncertain.
caveats: No six-digit occupation mix for the narrowed 541990 lens was found; the published 541900 occupation table also contains photographic and veterinary services.
The DOE examples are site-specific demonstrations and do not establish average labor savings across commercial firms.
The injected compensation ratio uses 2024 wages and 2022 receipts, so its level has a stated vintage mismatch outside this primitive.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time study across independent pipeline, utility, commodity, and fire-system inspection firms that separates current manual work from already-deployed drones, computer vision, and workflow software would materially change this value.
a.rationale: The exposed opportunity is concentrated in labor-intensive access and observation that drones can reduce, plus digital-image review, document comparison, scheduling, and report production that AI can accelerate. It is limited by field presence, testing-device operation, mechanical judgment, exception handling, and certification-grade review. The estimate excludes remote and sensor workflows already deployed today.

d5.bridge: EY forecasts the broader global testing, inspection, and certification market, while PHMSA documents a large, stable US pipeline installed base and stronger integrity-management inspection requirements. I convert that evidence to modest real growth in the current US physical-asset inspection basket, reducing the global nominal outlook for inflation, differences in geography and service scope, and the maturity of domestic pipeline mileage. The proxy may overstate growth if customers internalize inspections or infrastructure spending slows, and may understate it if regulation, extreme weather, grid expansion, and aging assets accelerate inspection frequency.
caveats: Pipeline mileage is an installed-base measure, not purchased outsourced inspection quantity.
The EY outlook is global, broader than the lens, and nominal rather than constant-price demand.
The narrowed lens spans end markets with different construction and maintenance cycles.
d5.change_evidence: Segment-level US revenue and inspection-unit histories for pipeline, power-line, commodity, and fire-system providers, deflated by service prices and paired with customer maintenance budgets and regulatory inspection calendars, would materially change this value.
d5.rationale: A persistent installed base and safety regulation support repeat inspection demand even without rapid construction growth. PHMSA requires post-extreme-weather inspections and stronger integrity assessments, while broader testing and inspection demand benefits from regulation, infrastructure investment, and quality requirements. Cyclicality in energy, construction, and commodity volumes keeps the downside close to current real demand.

caveats: The Census industry definition lists activities but does not measure their establishment or revenue shares.
The injected count of 863 is estimated using a broad Business and Consumer Services margin bridge and may not represent the margin profile of inspection firms.
Firms may use 541990 for government contracting even when their underlying activity fits another industry, increasing classification noise.
e.change_evidence: A commercial establishment roster matched to 541990 tax records and coded by primary activity, recurring revenue, customer type, ownership independence, normalized EBITDA, and contract transferability would directly establish the eligible share.
e.rationale: The Census definition confirms that visual pipeline and power-line inspection, commodity inspection, and fire-suppression testing belong in 541990, but the same code also contains many unrelated and often episodic services. A material minority of the estimated lower-middle-market population is therefore expected to fit the narrowed recurring inspection lens, with the wide range reflecting the absence of a firm-level activity mix.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541990 ($27,219,781,409) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541990 ($35,548,824,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $261,287 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541990); class <5: 18,002 firms, avg revenue/firm $408,515 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $63,933 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 1,791 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,600,634 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $250,499 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 911 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,055,095 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $478,122 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 717 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,186,007 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,437,610 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 146 firms, avg revenue/firm $36,320,979 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,684,233 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 123 firms, avg revenue/firm $86,630,772 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $13,557,716 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 863

o.bridge: BLS describes a related inspector occupation in which remote inspection reduces headcount but certification, worksite examination, testing-device use, mechanical knowledge, detailed findings, and written accountability remain. PHMSA and DOE show drones and AI changing how evidence is collected and reviewed rather than eliminating the inspection obligation. I translate this to the share of year-five service quantity still requiring an accountable inspection provider. Public-sector licensing evidence may overstate formal accountability in some private 541990 niches, while safety-critical utility work may require even more operator oversight.
caveats: Construction and building inspectors are not the same population as 541990 infrastructure and commodity inspectors.
Some routine visual patrol quantity may become fully automated or self-served by asset owners.
Operator need is highest in safety-critical regulated work and lower in low-consequence repeat observations.
o.change_evidence: Customer procurement policies, regulator guidance, insurer requirements, and service contracts specifying whether automated outputs may be accepted without human review or third-party responsibility would materially change the operator-required share.
o.rationale: Sensors, drones, and computer vision can replace travel, access labor, and first-pass image review, but customers still need a party to plan the inspection, validate sensor coverage and model output, investigate anomalies, apply standards, maintain records, and stand behind the conclusion. BLS expects remote inspection to reduce inspector demand rather than eliminate the occupation.

caveats: No source directly measures five-year retention of AI-derived benefits for independent US inspection providers.
Pricing power varies between emergency or specialized work and competitively bid routine patrols.
The global large-company cost pass-through evidence may overstate lower-middle-market bargaining power.
q.change_evidence: A representative contract and invoice sample showing pricing unit, renewal timing, escalation and benchmarking clauses, customer concentration, technician compensation, rebid outcomes, and gross-margin changes after technology deployment would materially change this estimate.
q.rationale: Per-visit, per-asset, route, and fixed-work-order pricing can let an operator retain near-term savings from faster data capture, image triage, and report production. Retention decays as customers rebid contracts, demand productivity concessions, compare technology-enabled providers, or bring remote monitoring in-house. High labor intensity and technician incentive needs also share part of the benefit internally.

rho.bridge: DOE and PHMSA demonstrate remote inspection, multisensor data capture, and deep-learning image processing in public infrastructure settings, while EY discusses AI adoption in the broader global testing, inspection, and certification sector. I translate demonstrated technical feasibility into a five-year implementation share for smaller US inspection firms, then haircut for equipment integration, data quality, aviation and site access, false-positive risk, customer approval, and workforce change. Demonstration and large-company evidence likely overstates near-term implementation for lower-middle-market firms.
caveats: Research pilots and large global testing companies have more technical resources than typical firms in the lens.
Implementation rates will differ sharply by asset accessibility, inspection modality, and customer regulation.
rho.change_evidence: A cohort study of lower-middle-market inspection firms reporting workflow-level deployment, capital cost, model validation, customer acceptance, realized hours saved, and exception rates after a full inspection cycle would materially change this value.
rho.rationale: Remote capture and automated image analysis are operational rather than purely speculative, and inspection providers face strong safety and productivity incentives. Implementation remains constrained by fragmented asset data, heterogeneous field conditions, sensor capital, proof-of-accuracy requirements, and the need to integrate outputs into regulated customer workflows.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year sale or transfer intentions for US employer-business owners across industries, while EY documents sustained acquisition activity in the broader global testing, inspection, and certification market. I haircut the employer-owner intention rate because intentions may not close, transfers may be internal or non-control, and small specialist firms can depend on owner credentials and relationships. Active sector consolidation partly offsets that haircut. The proxy likely overstates completed qualifying transfers if the narrowed firms are especially owner-dependent.
caveats: Gallup covers all US employer businesses rather than inspection firms and measures plans rather than completed transactions.
EY covers the global testing, inspection, and certification sector, which includes larger laboratories and certification businesses outside the lens.
The eligible population is only an estimated subset of the injected industry count.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census from PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, and specialist brokers, matched to the eligible US firm roster and distinguishing control acquisitions from internal transfers, would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: Employer-owner succession creates a plausible supply of firms, and inspection markets have strategic and sponsor acquisition interest. Actual control availability is lower than stated intent because credentials, customer approvals, contract assignment, and key-person knowledge can prevent a qualifying close.

principal_driver: Remote sensing and AI can remove substantial access, review, and reporting effort while safety and compliance obligations keep inspection demand recurring.
principal_weakness: The residual NAICS code is so heterogeneous that the eligible inspection population and the relevance of the injected industry-wide inputs are unusually uncertain.
